text:
NBA



USA Basketball managing director Grant Hill said he expects to have the12 NBA players selectedto represent the American side at the Paris Olympics by the “early portion of the playoffs,” which means late April or early May.

It’s an audacious goal, to have Team USA’s men’s basketball roster set with more than a month left in theNBA’s postseason; it’s too much time for a player Hill picked to be injured or otherwise tire out — especially considering theOlympicsare earlier this year and training camp begins in early July.

Advertisement

When Hill (in conjunction with coach Steve Kerr and general manager Sean Ford) have the USA team set, apparently this spring, Hill said, “There will be some people who are disappointed, who won’t make that final 12 roster” — an acknowledgement of the unprecedented pool of established NBA superstars, with unimpeachable international bona fides from past Team USA tours, mixed with younger, up-and-coming, American “pipeline” players who have expressed interest.



Indeed, when the likes ofKevin Durant(most decorated male USA player ever),LeBron James(arguably the greatest NBA player, with two Olympic golds),Stephen Curry(no Olympic roster, but two FIBA World Cup golds and father of the proliferation of the 3-point shot),Jayson TatumandDevin Booker(two stars in their prime, two key contributors to the last Olympic gold-winning team, whose styles are perfect for the international game) andJoel Embiid(no international experience but a 3-point shooting, low-post banging 7-footer and reigning NBA MVP) all say they’re in, they take up half the roster so long as they remain committed.

Given how badly USA Basketball has been exposed on the glass and defensively overall in the last two World Cups, past Olympic gold winnersAnthony DavisandBam Adebayowould figure to be shoe-ins to give the Americans a chance to match the size and physicality of France, Serbia and World Cup-champion Germany, among others. But that leaves four more spots.

Past Olympic gold winnersJames Harden,Jrue Holiday,Kyrie Irving,Damian LillardandChris Paulhave all expressed interest. If Team USA deep thinkers took them too, we’d have a set American roster without a single player from the World Cup last summer or from an impressive mix of NBA veteran superstars (Kawhi Leonard) and up-and-coming stars (Desmond Bane,Chet Holmgren) on the team. Oh, andJimmy Butler, one of the best NBA playoff performers in the last decade, who also has won Olympic gold, would be out, as wouldPaul George— another gold winner.

Advertisement

And what of players who aren’t considered to be top-level NBA stars, but who can fill a role, likeCavalierscenterJarrett Allen(a Kerr favorite who, admittedly, does have an All-Star appearance to his name and is a shot blocker),Derrick White(gritty on-ball defender whose offensive prowess has grown) orBobby Portis(screener, 3-point shooter and capable defender as a big)?

“In a perfect world, you’d like to have some balance,” Hill said Wednesday in a news conference followingthe unveiling of the 41-player poolfrom which Hill will pick the next Team USA. “It’s ultimately about winning, but you also want to continue with the pipeline and continue cultivating young players in the program. And, so, there’s a lot of forces there, and you’re trying to sort of balance it all.

“And I’ll say it’s a team. It’s making sure that we have incredible talent, but we also have pieces that fit and give us a chance against everything we’ll see during this experience,” Hill continued.

Hill said every player in the pool expressed an interest in playing for the Americans this summer. With the difficult or — conversationally speaking — impossible choices that lay ahead in taking the USA list from 41 to 12, there shouldn’t be too much hand-wringing over anyone who didn’t make the initial list — or even over a few surprises who did.

If a player wasn’t in the initial pool, their chances of making it to Paris were long anyway. And as for surprises who made it to the first pool, as of now, there doesn’t appear to be many spots available to hand out. Their inclusion was as much about keeping them engaged for future USA international appearances as their potential inclusion in the Paris Games.

The headliner not in the USA pool wasDraymond Green. The longtimeGolden State Warriorsforward, with two Olympic golds and who plays in the NBA for Kerr, had expressed a desire to play in Paris, and his game as a rebounder, five-position defender and passer fits.

Advertisement

Green also is battling through the most tumultuous season of his career, which included a lengthy suspension and trip to counseling for multiple instances of violent outbursts during games.

“We at USA basketball, we wanted to support him on his journey, and we just didn’t feel that playing over the summer gives him the best opportunity to do what he needs to do,” Hill said.

Bradley Bealwas originally on the Tokyo Olympics roster but was knocked off the team when he contracted COVID-19 during training camp. He is not in the USA pool this time. Nor isZion Williamson, who is enjoying a bit of a renaissance this season,Ja Morant, who is lost for the season due to shoulder surgery, orZach LaVine, who’s battled injuries this season but also was a member of the Tokyo Olympic gold-winning team.

Hill mentioned two more young, rising stars —Cade CunninghamandJalen Williams— who were not in the pool but perhaps could have been.

Among the potential surprises in the pool wasHawksstar guardTrae Young. Young is enjoying another All-Star-caliber season but has, over the last five years, raised his hand for USA consideration three times (two World Cups and the Tokyo Olympics) and has never been picked.

There have been concerns about Young’s slight build and his struggles defending on the perimeter that have hurt his cause, and Young couldn’t get a look for the World Cup roster last summer even though Hill is a minority owner of the Hawks — so, they already have a relationship. On Wednesday, Hill suggested that Young’s frustration over not being picked, and the way he handled it, led to his placement in the pool for the Paris Games.

“I think he’ll be somebody we’ll consider,” Hill said of Young. “But one thing I did tell him, and I will say this, I thought he was disappointed, obviously, last year, the fact that he was (so public about his disappointment) show a tremendous amount of courage. A lot of guys might not be so public, and he was very honest about his disappointment. And, so, that, in part, is why he’s been so good. He’s courageous, but we’ll see.”

(Top photo: Alex Slitz / Getty Images)

Joe Vardonis a senior NBA writer for The Athletic, based in Cleveland.Follow Joe on Twitter@joevardon
title: Why Draymond Green is not in the USA Basketball pool for Paris, but Trae Young is - The Athletic
author: Joe Vardon
description: Over the next few weeks, Grant Hill and company will take their list from 41 names down to 12.
keywords: Atlanta Hawks, Golden State Warriors, NBA, Olympics, Top Sports News
